result: I’ve been thinking about how the moon looks like it’s following me when I walk at night, a silver coin sliding across its own reflection in puddles. I used to believe it was a private escort appointed by the universe when I was seven and couldn’t fall asleep unless the curtains were open wide enough for one eye of light to watch over me. Now I know it’s just physics—angles and orbital mechanics—but the feeling lingers, the way the smell of mint toothpaste can still summon the dentist’s chair and the reassuring weight of my mother’s hand on my sternum. Memory obeys no torque equations; it slips sideways, lunar, tugging tides of emotion without logic. Last week I found a mixtape I recorded in 1999, labeled in glitter pen, and when I played it the ribbon snapped, releasing a confetti of brown oxide that stuck to the wet road like instant rust. The songs are gone, yet I can still hear the hiss between tracks, the anticipation sharper than any melody. Maybe that’s what we actually keep: the hush before the note, the moon’s breath on the back of our necks, the trembling instant before the cassette clicks play and everything is possible and nothing is yet lost.
